This looks fine, just one thing to note though:

The ui urls and the service api urls are diverging a little bit, but they were already inconsistent. We don't have a formal interface around ui urls, which is something we may wish to address in future, so that people and systems can safely link to ui elements.

The queue.getArtifact URL path is:
`<ROOT_URL>/api/queue/v1/task/<TASK_ID>/runs/<RUN_ID>/artifacts/<ARTIFACT_PATH>`

The Task Inspector URL path for the live log artifact is now:
`<ROOT_URL>/tasks/<TASK_ID>/runs/<RUN_ID>/logs/live/<ARTIFACT_NAME>`

As you see, the path elements had already diverged (e.g. `task` -> `tasks`, `artifacts` -> `logs/live`).

Thanks @Irving-cl for the update. Looks much nicer.

Some smaller comments/suggestion below.

The main suggestion/point from me still is about parsing of TLV/sub-TLVs. I think it's important to write the parsing of TLV/sub-TLVs in a way that it stays compatible with any future extensions/changes (e.g. if new sub-TLVs are added or new fields are added in an existing TLV). We take care of this in other parts of OT dealing with TLVs (e.g., the network-data TLVs and subTLVs).

In the new link-metrics code, we seem to have some hard-coded assumptions:
- Some of the new TLV code assumes the TLV is valid if its length is smaller than some min expected value. Generally it should be the other way, i.e., a larger TLV should be considered valid (to allow for future additions to TLV definition).
- Parsing over sub-TLVs, the code currently seems to assume certain order and exact number of sub-TLVs to be present, it should be flexible and skip over any sub-TLVs that it does not know/support.

I tried providing some examples of existing/related code where we have similar patterns to show approaches/solution on how to handle them.

Looks ok. As far as I see there are a couple of use cases we would like to cover:

- When we release the package to PyPI we call `sentinelhub.config --clear` and then upload the package. So if `config.json` wouldn't exist then `sentinelhub.config --clear` would trigger its creation. Therefore `config.json` will always be released together with the package and anyone who istalls it with pip will have it.
- If a developer clones a git repo and install the package e.g. with `pip install -e .` then `config.json` won't be created automatically. But often during development it's convenient to have it so that you can just copy-paste credentials into it.

Because of the 2nd case I suggest adding the following function into `setup.py`

```Python
def try_create_config_file():
    """ After the package is installed it will try to trigger saving a config.json file
    """
    try:
        from sentinelhub.config import SHConfig
        SHConfig()
    except BaseException:
        pass
```
and calling it at the end after the `setup` function is called. I checked that this creates `config.json` automatically right after a package is installed.

Hey Brian,

I checked the full history of `taskcluster-proxy` in the monorepo, and `taskcluster-proxy` in its original repository, and this appears never to have been the behaviour of `taskcluster-proxy` in the past.

TLDR; I think this is the wrong place to make the fix.

I think whatever broke, broke somewhere else, and this fix may end up causing us more pain than it solves. For example, it looks like this will set the `Content-Type` header for requests that have no body, such as GET requests, which could introduce adverse affects in middleware or caching proxies etc. I think passing through only the headers that are sent, plus the Authorization header, is the correct approach, and I suspect whatever was calling taskcluster-proxy is the thing that broke, rather than taskcluster-proxy itself.

I realise this might sound like I'm splitting hairs, but it feels like this change will mask problems elsewhere and could lead to unexpected results. For example, calling code that doesn't set the header when it runs through the proxy, wouldn't work if it was pointed at the taskcluster endpoint directly. The clients should all set the `Content-Type` header when it is required, so if a client is broken and no longer setting it, we have a different problem that this change would mask, but would still exist if the same client was used calling a taskcluster service directly.

I think the next steps for us is to do a deep dive to understand what is not passing the Content-Type header in a request to the proxy (whether it is something in a task, something in docker-worker, or something in a taskcluster client) and hopefully we can isolate the component that introduces the issue, and find the commit that broke it, and hopefully find the smoking gun! ☁️ 🔫

Hi @AllanOXDi - lots of new work here, which is really exciting to see! There is some smoothing out to be done on a few parts of the interface, as well as with some of the CRUD functionality of the device syncing schedule itself. To start with the syncing, I've added an inline comment about editing, but also wanted to add a little bit more context. When I click on the edit button, I do see the page is created and loads, but there are some console errors and no contents of the page. I'm not sure if this is the step you were at, or if the goal was to have the "edit" feature fully working. If you want to have the edit feature fully working, it seems like there are two errors happening, one related to your Vuex, and one related to the endpoint returning a 404 error. I'd suggest just debugging one at a time (perhaps with some route adjustments to make sure the device ID is also getting passed, it seems like that might be part of the problem), and then seeing if you can bring both pieces together. As for the UI, there is some general work that can be done to help it match the figma even more closely, even though it's just small things. First up: - overall, the page text looks a little bit "crowded" here. You can look at the figma and perhaps add a little bit of spacing. In some places, if you are using just `{{ $tr('here's my string') }}` to add text inside of a `div` or a `span`, wrapping it in a `

` tag can add some default padding that makes it feel a little bit roomier. You may still need to add a `class` to that `

` and adjust the spacing, but that might be a place to start. - You can see that the contents shifts when the dropdown is opened, and that it's a bit hard to scroll. I think adding a default, fixed height to the main page container, rather than just having it the size of the content that is there (for example, making it always take up ~80% of the page) might be a good idea. What do you think @jtamiace? https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/17235236/219707498-b84f2035-002d-4e1f-87d3-4b5b8b381339.mp4 - Finally, this is a bit of a nitpick, but to save Jessica having to do it, the alignment on the left should all be in one line. One tool I use to help with "pixel perfect" UIs (well, they are pixel perfect in my mind, although Jessica and Tomiwa might have a different opinion) is a Chrome browser extension called "Grid Rule". If you use Chrome you can just search for it and add it on. Turn on the extension, and then you can "pull" lines from the top or the left, to check the alignment of various page elements. https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/17235236/219709118-11e1f1bc-d3f8-45d8-b63c-ee2fbb480348.mp4 On the main sync schedules page, I have two suggestions. - Add some kind of loading state. One option would be to have a loading state for the whole table. The other option would be to have a loading state just for the sync status, if the other pieces of the table (i.e. device name) wouldn't take an extra fetch. This would help reduce the time where it looks like there are no devices - I'm not sure why there are four statuses here. I tried to figure it out from the code and it seems like `myDevices` is being set to `tasks` - if there is one task per device, that would be right, but here, it seems like there might be more than one task per device, in which case we may need to rethink how this is being displayed to the user. https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/17235236/219710099-7028e4ea-5454-4242-bb06-d4133da2714e.mp4 Finally - and this maybe is a question for @rtibbles regarding the expected behavior (maybe with @bjester weighing in if any of the refactoring work you are doing could relate to this?) I am now running into fun edge cases since I'm on ZeroTier and I can see other's devices available. This morning I saw both Samson's and Allan's computers in the "devices" that were on network. Trying to set a sync schedule on these (logically) resulted in a 403 error. Do we want to filter those devices out entirely? Do we want to filter them out for now, but in the future, add an option to input admin credentials in them, so that they could sync? Just wanted to raise this so we can start thinking about it. Okay - that's all I have for now, but let me know any questions you have or where you might need more tips or details, and I will do my best to help! Great progress on this!

I appreciate the PR and ended up using it myself to test a server in another region.  However there are a few changes I'd suggest.

The current PR puts most of `get_servers()` under a conditional (`if servers_json is None:`), which can possibly break other functionality and makes the diff harder to analyze since things are nested a step further.

I would suggest:

- Change the command line flag to something simpler like just `--json [file.json]`
- Instead of --json overriding get_servers(), have it add the server to the servers list. Instead of the conditional, rearrange it like:
```
#start of get_servers()
"""If it exists, load in a server config from a JSON file."""
if servers_json:
    printer('Loading Servers from:\n%s' % servers_json, debug=True)
    try:
        with open(servers_json) as json_file:
            self.servers = json.load(json_file)
    except FileNotFoundError:
        #handle the exception
#continue with get_servers()
if servers is None:
    #...
```
Then the user can still specify the server via `--server [ID]`.  The reason I suggest this over the current implementation is that there may be cases where the user wants to include the server or servers via JSON but not exclude other servers from being considered relative to "best server based on ping".

39d0101, after some other minor adjustments, magically made the test suite succeed, by adjusting the parameter mangling to/from [`crate_before_execute`](https://github.com/crate/crate-python/blob/0.26.0/src/crate/client/sqlalchemy/compiler.py#L73-L78).

Other than the inline comments, some other items could/should be addressed before finalizing this patch. Please let me know about your suggestions and if you can spot other shortcomings.

1. Special attention should be given to the new SQL Compilation Caching feature [1,2]. The `CrateDialect` should `supports_statement_cache = True`, see [3]. Also, there is a new `cache_ok` attribute on custom types [4], which should probably be applied to all CrateDB SQLAlchemy types listed in `crate.client.sqlalchemy.types`. [5] might also be related here.

   Currently, there is this warning:
   > SAWarning: Dialect crate:crate-python will not make use of SQL compilation caching as it does not set the `supports_statement_cache` attribute to `True`.  This can have significant performance implications including some performance degradations in comparison to prior SQLAlchemy versions.  Dialect maintainers should seek to set this attribute to True after appropriate development and testing for SQLAlchemy 1.4 caching support.   Alternatively, this attribute may be set to False which will disable this warning. (Background on this error at: https://sqlalche.me/e/14/cprf)

2. Go through the list of significant changes [6] thoroughly and summarize/compile relevant details regarding CrateDB into the changelog file.

3. Test Asynchronous I/O with `asyncpg`, see [7].
   ```
   engine = create_async_engine(
     "postgresql+asyncpg://scott:tiger@localhost/test"
   )
   ```

[1] https://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/14/core/connections.html#sql-compilation-caching
[2] https://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/14/faq/performance.html#faq-new-caching
[3] https://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/14/core/connections.html#caching-for-third-party-dialects
[4] https://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/14/core/custom_types.html
[5] https://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/14/errors.html#caching-caveats
[6] https://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/14/changelog/migration_14.html
[7] https://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/14/orm/extensions/asyncio.html

This seems like a huge amount of work, so thanks for that, @drmoose!

However, this is also quite a bit of code, and I'm not entirely convinced it should be added to pybind11. But please do try to convince me (and others), @drmoose and @bstaletic.

A few questions that first come to mind, when seeing this:
- `sys.argv` can be set directly, I believe (through `py::module::import("sys").attr("argv") = ...`). What's the reason to call `PySys_SetArgvEx`?
- Why do we want to support the `char **argv, int argc` signature, and not just `std::vector<const char*>` or even `std::vector<std::string>`. I can see that C/C++'s `main` takes these arguments, of course, but wouldn't it almost always be bad practice to immediately pass on these arguments of `main` to Python? Wouldn't you, in a situation where Python is embedded, expect the main, embedding program to handle the arguments before deciding to pass on a few to Python? Or maybe the embedding program wants to just set a fixed set of arguments? If not, it's seems more like "wrapping" CPython, rather than embedding it. It's also kind of annoying to produce a `char**`, when you're not getting it as `main` parameters (but from `char**` to `std::vector<std::string>` is reasonably straightforward).
- This argument is further strengthened by the `updatepath` in `PySys_SetArgvEx`, which tries out whether `argv[0]` is a script file (which, if you're passing on `main`s arguments, is wrong, because it'll be the executable's name?). This also somehow doesn't sense to me, except when you're thinly "wrapping" Python rather than embedding it in a larger program (and even then, not really, because it's the executable). The question here is what the default assumption should be; I'd say pybind11 should assume as little as possible, here, as long as it's possible for the user to achieve this if necessary.
- Why is `Py_DecodeLocale` being used; what advantage does it offer? I'd say it's up to the embedding program to decide on the encoding, rather than use Python's defaults here? Shouldn't we stick to pybind11's ["utf-8 everywhere"](https://pybind11.readthedocs.io/en/stable/advanced/cast/strings.html#passing-python-strings-to-c)?
- Do we really want to set `sys.argv == ['']` by default? I'm actually fine with that, but this was once rejected by @wjakob (https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/issues/1768#issuecomment-503474136). See also discussions in #1768 and #2061.
- On a related note: does this need to be part of the constructor? (For some reason, the order of the arguments seems funky to me, thought I know it's to keep backwards compatibility with the API without these arguments, ofc). Couldn't this be a method?
Thank you for folliwing up!

Per discussion: it turns out that we don’t actually need this code for
the sequence of work that we’re planning on checking in. We need to
render a chart with an *x* axis, a *y* axis, a set of grid lines, and
the actual data series. There are two approaches:

 1. Render all elements in an `OffscreenCanvas` in a worker thread.
 2. Render the data series in an `OffscreenCanvas`, render the others in
    SVG, and composite them together with normal CSS grid in an Angular
    component.

We’re proceeding with option (2) because it’s simpler and gives better
output (3D canvas text rendering is weird), which means that we don’t
need our own layout engine at all.

For others interested in understanding better the motivation behind this PR, I suggest https://docs.nvidia.com/deploy/pdf/CUDA_Compatibility.pdf .  One paragraph worth repeating from that doc is:
```
To use other CUDA APIs introduced in a minor release (that require a new
driver), one would have to implement fallbacks or fail gracefully. This situation
is not different from what is available today where developers use macros to
compile out features based on CUDA versions. Users should refer to the CUDA
headers and documentation for new CUDA APIs introduced in a release.
```
Thus, it's fair to use an 11.1 feature that is supported by both 11.1 and 11.0 kernel-mode drivers.  Before using an 11.1 feature that requires an 11.1 kernel-mode driver, one should check dynamically for that feature's presence at runtime, as suggested in the document section 3.2 "Handling New CUDA Features."  This is particularly important to pay attention to while the upstream CI testing has no enhanced-compatibility build.

There's a potential race condition here where a span is started after the writer is stopped but before the start method is changed.

I am inclined to have this check handled by `AgentWriter.write`. 

Also, should this be more than just a warning? If the writer service has been stopped, spans should not be queued up for writing. However, we do not distinguish between a service stopped and a service that has not yet been started.
Thanks @pmeier , approving to unblock but I have minor comments
@richardliaw @ericl @rkooo567 please review it before I add tests.

> Given that we do not require a forksafe lock here

Worth noting that we don't require a fork safe lock because the lock in the context objects are recreated on fork: https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-py/blob/a1932e8ddb704d259ea8a3188d30bf542f59fd8d/ddtrace/tracer.py#L489-L508 when starting a new span.

However, there is the possibility that a deadlock could occur still if a span from a parent process is used in a child and there is a competing thread that acquires the context lock when the fork occurs. In this case the context lock in the child will be permanently locked and if the user tries to update the sampling priority on the span (and context) from the parent process then a dead lock will occur.

Can we document that spans should only be used in the process in which they are created? Maybe in the Span docstring and in the context section: https://ddtrace.readthedocs.io/en/v0.56.1/advanced_usage.html#span

Thanks @oke-aditya for the PR. Given that this was originally discussed with @NicolasHug, we should wait for his review. 

In general though, I'm not sure why we want to have a random color palette by default. I fully agree it is better than using only white for everything, but I'm not sure why randomizing the colors is better than fixed ones. Apart from the non-determinism, by generating the colors at random we also risk using very similar colors for different classes, which can be confusing. It is actually not easy to have multiple colors that are easily distinguishable by humans. I suggest we hardcode color maps such as [this one](https://colorbrewer2.org/#type=qualitative&scheme=Paired&n=12). It "only" defines colors for 12 classes. If we need more, we can always supplement with randomly generated afterwards.

> We're using the local storage to store downloaded Artifacts. Does Dagster always run with a local filesystem that we can use? And what would happen when there is no more disk space?

dagster is always running with a local filesystem, but when it's deployed in a remote setup, for example, each run could be in its own container which means local filesystem will be ephemera in a run scope, i.e. files downloaded to run 1's local filesystem won't be available to the next run.

i think it's ok to default to local filesystem, but it'd be great to give an option for users to download to other filesystems, e.g. an option to hook to an s3 bucket. -- but this isn't blocking this PR IMO. we can add it in a later iteration.

I understand that this PR brings a few different sets of changes:

1. Refactor of the way requirements are declared: from arrays in the `setup.py` to separate files in the `requirements` folder.
2. Pinning down all non-default requirements versions.
3. Supporting constraining the `numpy` version.
4. Change the supported Python version from 3.6 and 3.7 to 3.7, 3.8 and 3.9.
5. Refactor all CI-specific dependencies to the standard requirements declaration.

If this is correct, then:

- I am very much in support of (5).
- While in favour of it, I don't understand how this changeset enables (4). I believe we should add matrix testing in CI if we want to support multiple Python versions.
- I don't understand the expected usage of (3): if we advertise support for multiple `numpy` versions, why do we test only against the oldest version?
- I don't understand the added value of (2), while I see the cost of needing to deploy a new Core version whenever a patch is published in one of the dev requirements 😰
- I am not sure I understand the added value of (1). I like how each set of requirements has its own file, but I also like that all requirements are in `setup.py`. What is the most standard in Python dependency management?

Hey @oke-aditya and sorry for the delay. Apart from your questions I've added two more comments inline.

> ```
> torchvision/models/quantization/utils.py:31: error: Incompatible types in assignment (expression has type "QConfig", variable has type "Union[Tensor, Module]")  [assignment]
>             model.qconfig = torch.quantization.QConfig(
>                             ^
> ```

The ignore is justified, because we are adding an attribute to a live object and `mypy` can't deal with that.

> ```
> torchvision/models/quantization/utils.py:35: error: "Tensor" not callable  [operator]
> model.fuse_model()
>         ^
> ```

`mypy` complains, because it correctly can't find a callable `fuse_model` parameter on an `nn.Module`. Thus, we need make the input type annotation more precise. I'm guessing we only accepting the quantized models that we have built-in? If yes, we could create a custom class mixin (for example `QuantizedModuleMixin`) that also inherits from `nn.Module` and adds this as abstract method. All our models could inherit from making `fuse_model` also available for checking. 

> The cat cat errors
> 
> ```
> torchvision/models/quantization/shufflenetv2.py:33: error: Argument 1 to "cat" of "FloatFunctional" has incompatible type "Tuple[Tensor, Any]"; expected "List[Tensor]"  [arg-type]
>                 out = self.cat.cat((x1, self.branch2(x2)), dim=1)
>                                     ^
> torchvision/models/quantization/shufflenetv2.py:35: error: Argument 1 to "cat" of "FloatFunctional" has incompatible type "Tuple[Any, Any]"; expected "List[Tensor]"  [arg-type]
>                 out = self.cat.cat((self.branch1(x), self.branch2(x)), dim=1)
>                                     ^
> ```

There are actually two errors here:

1. We are passing a tuple when a list is expected. We should be able to replace the parentheses with brackets: `(x1, self.branch2(x2))` to `[x1, self.branch2(x2)]`.
2. `self.branch*()` has no proper return type, so we need to cast ourselves: `self.branch2(x2)` to `cast(torch.Tensor, self.branch2(x2))`

> 
> The init errors, this probably has a nice workaround
> 
> ```
> torchvision/models/quantization/inception.py:111: error: "__init__" of "InceptionA" gets multiple values for keyword argument "conv_block"  [misc]
>             super(QuantizableInceptionA, self).__init__(conv_block=QuantizableBasicConv2d, *args, **kwargs)
>             ^
> 
> torchvision/models/quantization/inception.py:184: error: "__init__" of "InceptionAux" gets multiple values for keyword argument "conv_block"  [misc]
>             super(QuantizableInceptionAux, self).__init__(conv_block=QuantizableBasicConv2d, *args, **kwargs)
>             ^
> ```

I don't think so, because we currently make the hard assumption that no one passes `conv_block` as keyword argument. If someone would do, we would get an error at runtime, because we also pass it.

If we insist on using `**kwargs` here, I'm afraid there is no clean way to do it. One workaround would be to add a `conv_block: None = None` before the `**kwargs` and bail out if we encounter anything else than `None`. cc @fmassa @datumbox 

Note that there are a lot more of these in the [CI failure](https://app.circleci.com/pipelines/github/pytorch/vision/9701/workflows/c3ab1c23-934f-49dd-98bf-6d5991c01648/jobs/720421).

> Assigning a layer of model to None error
> 
> ```
> torchvision/models/quantization/googlenet.py:81: error: Incompatible types in assignment (expression has type "None", variable has type Module)  [assignment]
>                 model.aux1 = None
>                              ^
> ```

Porbably fine, because we do the same thing in the regular `GoogLeNet`. 

https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/db530d8c619a52753f4ce5207af7083162b4228e/torchvision/models/googlenet.py#L56-L57 

Not sure if this is a jit issue, but maybe we can type them as `Optional[nn.Module]` in the first place?

Thank you, @sairina . The overall direction is looking great. I have two high-level suggestions for now that I think would be good to address.

For later reference, the  `NotificationsRoot` is visually responsible for the following four components which I'll call the messaging components

| `AuthMessage` | `AppError` | `GlobalSnackbar` | `UpdateNotification` |
| -------------------- | -------------- | ----------------------- | --------------------------- |
| ![AuthMessage](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/13509191/156009457-d918fbe9-0ceb-40b8-8281-4d495788c9f1.png) | ![AppError](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/13509191/156009448-ca417992-744e-4af2-97e9-5aa9c9aa62d3.png)  | ![GlobalSnackbar](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/13509191/156009461-6488be67-7021-485d-9197-443deda639eb.png) | ![UpdateNotification](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/13509191/156009462-9a5f0118-dc6f-4e3f-8f61-925b75d1c307.png) |

### (1) Decoupling of unnecessary style and layout information (including deletion of related props)

 I’d suggest removing all styles that are not directly related to the messaging components. That’s the majority of computed styles (e.g. `mainWrapperStyles` , `contentStyles`) as well as styles defined in `<style>` block. It’d be fine to keep styles that are unnecessary to position these components on a page if there are some. This also applies to related props: `fullScreen` , `hasSidebar` ,`maxMainWidth` , and `marginBottom`.

**Reason**

`NotificationsRoot` is planned to be used in the following way from plugin index pages:

```javascript
// LearnIndex

<template>
  <NotificationsRoot>
    <router-view />
  </NotificationsRoot>
</template>
```

One of the points of `CoreBase` refactor is to allow each page of the plugin that’s rendered in the `<router-view />` to have full control over its layout, including margins, padding, etc. to gain more flexibility. That wouldn’t be fully possible if we kept unnecessary layout and style dependencies in the `NotificationsRoot`.

### (2) Decoupling loading state from the default slot

I’d suggest removing  `v-if="!loading"` from [the whole wrapper div here](https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/pull/9128/files#diff-4a205996a86d148f2aeb0e7a84a2f6ea6325fe25f0f548eae3230a4390abdce4R9) and only using it directly on components that need it, that’s `AuthMessage` and `AppError` . For example

```javascript
<KPageContainer v-if="!loading && notAuthorized">
  <AuthMessage
    :authorizedRole="authorizedRole"
    :header="authorizationErrorHeader"
    :details="authorizationErrorDetails"
  />
</KPageContainer>
```

**Reason**

Keeping `v-if="!loading"` on the div that’s also wrapping [the default slot](https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/pull/9128/files#diff-4a205996a86d148f2aeb0e7a84a2f6ea6325fe25f0f548eae3230a4390abdce4R33) has unhappy consequences. Consider the situation demonstrated in the following example when we’d try to set loading state from one of the pages of the Learn plugin:

```javascript
// LearnIndex

<template>
  <NotificationsRoot>
    <router-view />
  </NotificationsRoot>
</template>
```

```javascript
// Library (rendered within the <router-view /> of LearnIndex)

<script>

export default {
  created() {
    state.loading = true
    fetchData().then(() => state.loading = false)
  }
}

</script>
```

At the moment we set `state.loading = true` in the `created` hook, the Library component would get destroyed (therefore `state.loading = false` would never get called) if we kept `v-if="!loading"` as is in the `NotificationsRoot` . This would make all logic around the loading state rigid and difficult to refactor later (and debug - true story :)

---

To sum it up, this means we’d only keep things that are absolutely unnecessary for notifications and messaging components in the `NotificationsRoot`  (and if we find out we need something more from it in the future, we can do that when a need arises while considering architecture and alternative options to solving that particular need).

Sorry for the large number of comments. Took me half-way through before I had most of the details here figured out.

The main theme concludes to:
- I think that we want to swap the order of declaring defaults so we can do
```python
default_requirements = ["proj>=X.Y,<Z", "other", "stuff"]
default_version = default_requirements[0]
default_extra_requirements = default_requirements[1:]
```
Then we are in a good position to remove `default_version` and `default_extra_requirements`.

- I take it we employ a "default policy" of floating versions on a single major. Makes sense to me. Exceptions are to be expected, but when we find them, I think we would do well to document them.
 
- This is a large enough change already, so this may be for another PR. This line struck out at me:
```python 
 default_interpreter_constraints = ["CPython>=3.7,<4"]
```

At the very least we may want to move our "default default_interpreter_constraints" value to a CONST for re-use to DRY this up a bit.

Thanks for adding this! I have a few comments, some more high level:

1. The current implementation effectively constraints the first t beam search steps to the target prefix, which is neat and probably sufficient for most use cases where the target prefix is short. I wonder though if there would be value in thinking about how we could implement this with code from the scoring modules, the advantage being that computation over time steps within the target prefix could be parallelized. This would likely be a bit more involved (also because of JIT compilation etc.), but just wanted to hear your thoughts on this.
2. Since you are technically scoring the target prefix, can you create an integration/system test that asserts on output and score equality between: (a) scoring some data and (b) translating the source-sides data with the target-side encoded as target prefixes (including `</s>`?)? The existing integration/system tests already do this to some extent: we score the inputs and outputs from a previous beam search and assert that the scores are equal: https://github.com/awslabs/sockeye/blob/ea081433d0058c646da247412812c421012c0e43/test/common.py#L108
3. Do we need to add support for target factors?
